Home Day 1 — Intro & Film Day 2 — Atomic Art Day 3 — Deterrence Day 4 — Activism Day 5 — Action Plan Resources
This is a live issue — not Cold War history

Understanding nuclear risk & your power to act

A one-week course built around How to Stop a Nuclear War. No prior knowledge needed — just curiosity and a willingness to engage.

5 days · college level Visual · discussion · hands-on Emotional check-ins included
!
Why now? In 2025, India-Pakistan tensions reached their highest point in decades. Nine countries still hold ~12,500 nuclear warheads. This week connects those facts to what you can actually do.
Watch the documentary trailer
A note before you begin

In this curriculum we're discussing nuclear weapons, current military practices, and active conflicts — not as distant history, but as systems that are operating right now. Some of this material may feel heavy or unsettling. That's normal.

We're not here to overwhelm you or leave you feeling helpless. We're here because we take you seriously — seriously enough to share real information, and seriously enough to believe you can engage with complexity and find your own way to respond.

What we're asking

Stay curious. Engage honestly with the material. Ask hard questions.

What we're offering

Evidence-based information, space to think critically, and pathways from understanding to action.

Day 1 — Introduction & film screening

4 activities·Est. 5 hrs·No prior knowledge needed
Overview
Pre-reading
Film screening
Discussion
Reflection
Why today matters

The issue isn't history — it's now

Most students arrive thinking nuclear war is a Cold War relic. Today, using the documentary and its context, we challenge that assumption together. By the end of Day 1 you'll understand how risk accumulates — and why your voice matters.

Today's sequence
Pre-reading — Ch. 1 of The Button by Perry & Collina
Film screening — How to Stop a Nuclear War (~90 min)
Discussion — Small group + full class on the film's arguments
Reflection — Anonymous poll + written check-in
Key concepts introduced today

Nuclear deterrence

The logic that having nukes prevents their use — and why systems failures challenge this assumption.

Risk by accident

Most close calls were errors and miscommunication, not aggression. 1983's Able Archer nearly triggered a launch.

Student agency

The 1980s freeze movement shows students have moved nuclear policy before. This course is about what comes next.

Pre-reading

"The danger is not that our leaders are evil. The danger is that the system we have built gives very little room for error — and in a crisis, error is almost guaranteed."

— William Perry & Tom Collina, The Button (2020), Ch. 1 (paraphrased for discussion)

Reading: The Button — Chapter 1

Read pages 1–18. Focus on Perry's account of the moments when he, as Secretary of Defense, came close to recommending a nuclear strike based on false information. Pay attention to: how the decision was made, who was in the room, and how much time they had.

5-minute silent free write — after reading

What part of the reading surprised, interested, or confused you?

Film screening
♡
Before you watch: Some imagery in this film may be difficult. It's okay to step away if needed. The facilitator will check in at the timestamp below — you don't have to engage with every moment.

Trailer shown above. Your facilitator will screen the full documentary during class time.

Key timestamp to watch for — 00:18:40Able Archer 1983: how a NATO exercise nearly triggered a Soviet nuclear response — and what stopped it.
During viewing — jot notes as you watch

What moments surprised you? What questions came up?

Discussion — answer first, then talk with classmates

Answer each question on your own first. Once your class has answered, your facilitator will reveal results and open the floor for discussion.

Question 1 — comprehension

According to the film, what is the primary driver of nuclear risk today?

Deliberate first strikes by hostile states
Accidental launches or miscommunication under pressure
Terrorist acquisition of warheads
Breakdown of international inspection regimes
Now discuss with a classmate or small group

The film argues that accidental risk is greater than intentional risk. Do you agree? Consider:

  • What systems or protocols exist to prevent accidents?
  • Does the Able Archer example change how you think about deterrence?
  • What would have to change for you to feel safer?
Question 2 — analysis

The film frames nuclear risk as a systems problem rather than a political one. What does this mean for how we respond?

Only governments and militaries can fix it — individuals have little role
Changing public understanding can shift political will, which changes systems
Technology solutions (AI, automation) are the only realistic path
The problem is too complex for any single approach to address
Turn and talk — 3 minutes

Which answer do you find most compelling, and why? The film specifically highlights student activism as a lever for change. Does that feel realistic or idealistic to you?

  • What examples of student-driven change can you think of?
  • Where do you see your own role after this week?
♡
Check in with yourself first. It's okay if today brought up strong feelings. Take a breath. You can skip any question.
Anonymous poll
How are you feeling after today?

Choose the option that fits best — results are anonymous and shared with the class.

Motivated — I want to learn and do more
Surprised — I didn't know the risk was this current
Overwhelmed — this is a lot to take in
Skeptical — I want to see more evidence first
No discussion needed — just notice how the room feels together.

Your facilitator may acknowledge the results before moving on.

Exit reflection — 5 minutes

In 2–3 sentences: what surprised you most today, and why does it matter to you personally?

← Course home

Day 2 — Atomic Art in America

4 activities·Est. 3 hrs·Visual & discussion focused
Warm-up
NYT 1945 Article
Artwork 1 — Peterdi
Artwork 2 — Dehner
Artwork 3 — Moholy-Nagy
Discussion
Exit ticket
Warm-up — opening question
Opening discussion prompt

How has nuclear war been represented in media you've encountered? Consider films, TV, books, music, or visual art.

Some examples students often mention

The 100 (series), Oppenheimer (film), Planet of the Apes (series), Thirteen Days (book & film)

Your initial reaction

What emotion do these nuclear portrayals most often evoke in you?

Fear — they feel realistic and alarming
Fascination — I find the topic compelling
Apathy — it feels distant and unlikely
Confusion — I don't have enough context to feel strongly
Follow-up — turn and talk

Do these portrayals feel realistic or exaggerated? Do they make nuclear war feel distant or immediate? Share with a classmate before we look at how artists responded in 1945.

"We've been living under the threat of nuclear war for over 80 years. After the bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki in 1945, people grappled with uncertainty and fear. Much of the art from the 1940s–1960s turned toward abstraction as a means to express emotions that were difficult to put into words."

Abstraction: using distorted forms, surreal imagery, and biomorphic shapes to express the inexpressible.

Activity 1 — New York Times Article, August 12, 1945

"We Enter a New Era — the Atomic Age"

This article was published on August 12, 1945 — just six days after Hiroshima and three days after Nagasaki. This is how the atomic bombing was initially explained to the American public.

🔗 "We Enter a New Era — the Atomic Age"New York Times Magazine, August 12, 1945 — Harry M. Davis → nytimes.com
!
If time is limited: Read the opening through paragraph 5 ("…has startled all who pause to think"), then skip to paragraph 16 ("The magnitude of the powers thus put…") through to the end.
5-minute silent free write — after reading

What part of the article surprised, interested, or confused you?

Activity 2 — Artwork 1 of 3

Look at the artwork below first — without reading the context. Share your initial reactions before revealing the background.

Gabor Peterdi, Nervous Lobster, 1947. Oil on canvas, 34 × 44½"
Before you read anything: What do you see? What do you feel? What questions come up?

Gabor Peterdi was a Hungarian-American artist who studied in Italy before immigrating to the United States in 1939. He enlisted in the US Army and fought in Europe during World War II, then established and directed the Graphic Workshop at the Brooklyn Museum Art School. His work explores vulnerability and tension. This piece was created using etching and gaufrage (embossing on metal plates) on zinc.

Possible interpretations
  • The distorted lobster can be read as a symbol of nature's fragility under technological pressure
  • The mechanical quality reflects an unease about how technology and nature have become entangled
  • The red coloring signals anxiety about the increasing influence of technology on society
Activity 2 — Artwork 2 of 3
Dorothy Dehner, Woman #2, 1954. Watercolor and ink on paper, 22¾ × 18"
Look first — what do you see? What feelings or questions come up before you read anything?

Dorothy Dehner was an American painter and sculptor born in 1901 in Cleveland, Ohio, known for her abstract work. After her divorce in 1950, she became more professionally active and studied printmaking at William Stanley Hayter's Atelier 17. Her work after 1950 focused on individuals' personal stories in the Atomic Age.

Possible interpretations
  • Meant to express an internal psychological impact rather than an external threat
  • Interpreted by many as a human-centered response to the Atomic Age — survival and identity, not politics
  • The fragmented, looping forms may represent the tension between individual identity and social instability
Activity 2 — Artwork 3 of 3
László Moholy-Nagy, Nuclear II, 1946. Oil on canvas (dimensions to be confirmed)
Look first — what do you see? What do the shapes, colors, or composition make you feel?

László Moholy-Nagy was a Hungarian painter and photographer, heavily influenced by constructivism. He was a strong advocate for integrating technology into the arts. This abstract oil painting, composed of geometric shapes and muted colors, was made in response to the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki — while Moholy-Nagy was recovering from cancer.

Possible interpretations
  • A more structured and even optimistic reading of nuclear energy — technology as progress
  • The white squares and triangles suggest missile-like shapes beneath the geometry
  • The vibrant colors could celebrate nuclear technology as a source of renewal — but raises the question: can we separate nuclear power from nuclear weapons?
Discussion — art, journalism, and emotional truth

Answer individually first, then discuss with a partner before sharing with the full class.

Discussion question 1

In your view, is art or journalism more effective at conveying the emotional truth of an event like Hiroshima?

Art — it captures feeling and ambiguity that facts can't
Journalism — evidence and clarity build more lasting understanding
Both are needed — they work on different registers
Neither fully captures it — lived experience is irreplaceable
Full class discussion prompts
  • What is the overall tone and message of the 1945 NYT article?
  • How does that tone compare to the emotions in the artworks?
  • Why might media and artists portray the same event so differently?
  • Do you think nuclear war is over-sensationalized, underrepresented, or misunderstood in today's media?
Exit ticket
Option 1 — written reflection

After today's discussion, has your perception of nuclear war changed? How?

Option 2 — sketch

Draw a quick sketch of your interpretation of one of the concepts or artworks from today. Hand it to your facilitator as you leave.

← Course home

Day 3 — Hiroshima & Nagasaki: Decision, destruction & ethics

5 activities·Est. 4 hrs·Historical, visual & ethical
Overview
Truman's diary
Stimson memo
Human cost
Discussion & essay
!
Today's focus: On August 6 and 9, 1945, the United States dropped atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki. Today we examine why leaders made that decision — and what it actually meant for the people on the ground.
Today's sequence
Truman's diary entry — July 25, 1945: the decision in his own words
Stimson's memo — the strategic case made to Truman, July 1945
The human cost — Hiroshima Shadows and the Hiroshima Panels
Discussion & essay — ethics, decision-making, and what we carry forward
Key questions for today

Why did they decide to bomb?

What reasoning did Truman and his advisors give — and what does the historical record reveal about their priorities?

Who bore the cost?

The bombs killed an estimated 110,000–210,000 people, most of them civilians. How do we reckon with that?

What does this mean today?

Hiroshima and Nagasaki are not just history — they are the only two times nuclear weapons have been used in war. They define what is at stake.

Primary source — Truman's diary entry, July 25, 1945

Context

This diary entry was written by President Truman at the Potsdam Conference, two weeks before the bombing of Hiroshima. It is one of the most direct windows into how the decision was framed by the person who made it.

"We have discovered the most terrible bomb in the history of the world. It may be the fire destruction prophesied in the Euphrates Valley era, after Noah and his fabulous ark... This weapon is to be used against Japan between now and August 10th. I have told the secretary of war, Mr Stimson, to use it so that military objectives and soldiers and sailors are the target and not women and children. Even if Japs are savages, ruthless, merciless and fanatic, we as the leader of the world for the common welfare cannot drop this terrible bomb on the old capital or the new... It is certainly a good thing for the world that Hitler's crowd or Stalin's did not discover this atomic bomb. It seems to be the most terrible thing ever discovered, but it can be made the most useful."

— Harry S. Truman, diary entry, July 25, 1945, Potsdam Conference

🔗 Read the full diary entry → alphahistory.com
Reading response — 5 minutes

What assumptions does Truman make in this entry? What does he seem certain about — and what does he seem to be working to convince himself of?

Primary source — Secretary of War Henry Stimson's memo to Truman, July 1945

Context

This memo, classified Top Secret at the time, laid out the strategic rationale for using the bomb against Japan. It was written by Secretary of War Henry Stimson and addressed directly to President Truman approximately one month before Hiroshima.

📄
Stimson Memorandum — "Proposed Program for Japan," July 2, 1945 (Top Secret) Read the full declassified document at the National Security Archive.
🔗 Read the Stimson memo → nsarchive.gwu.edu
As you read, consider

Stimson frames the bombing as a way to avoid a land invasion that would cost hundreds of thousands of lives on both sides. How does he weigh civilian harm? What does he leave out? Compare his language to Truman's diary entry from July 25.

Comparative reading response

How does Stimson's memo differ in tone and framing from Truman's diary? What does each document reveal about how decision-makers thought about the bomb?

The human cost — Hiroshima shadows

When the atomic bomb detonated over Hiroshima, the thermal flash was so intense that people sitting or standing near stone surfaces were vaporized — leaving only their shadows burned permanently into the stone. These photographs are among the most haunting records of what nuclear weapons actually do.

Photograph by Yoshito Matsushige. A journalist's legs were included to provide context to the scene.

Taken in 1946. A Japanese man recreating the likely position of the person on the steps of the Sumitomo Bank whose shadow was burned into the stone when the atomic bomb was dropped.

The Hiroshima Panels

Iri and Toshi Maruki — The Hiroshima Panels (1950–1982)

Japanese artists Iri and Toshi Maruki spent over 30 years creating 15 large-scale paintings documenting the aftermath of the atomic bombings. The panels do not show the bomb — they show its survivors: bodies, grief, and the struggle to continue living. They are considered among the most important works of art produced in the 20th century.

🔗 View the Hiroshima Panels → marukigallery.jp
Reflection — 5 minutes

After seeing the shadow photographs and reading about the Hiroshima Panels: What do these images do that the documents (Truman's diary, Stimson's memo) cannot? What is the relationship between political decision-making and the human reality it produces?

Discussion question

Based on today's primary sources and images — do you believe the decision to bomb Hiroshima and Nagasaki was justified?

Yes — the strategic reasoning was sound and it shortened the war
No — deliberately targeting civilian populations is never justified
It's complicated — the context makes simple judgments difficult
I need more information before forming a view
Full class discussion

Consider: Truman wrote that he told Stimson to target "military objectives and soldiers and sailors — not women and children." The bombs killed an estimated 110,000–210,000 people, the vast majority civilians. How do we hold these two facts together? And what does it mean that we are still living under the same logic of deterrence today?

Short essay — graded
Essay prompt

In approximately 300 words: Using at least one primary source from today (Truman's diary or Stimson's memo) and one visual source (Hiroshima Shadows or the Maruki Panels), argue whether the decision to bomb Hiroshima and Nagasaki can be ethically defended. What does your answer imply for how we think about nuclear weapons today?

Draft your essay here
← Course home

Day 4 — Activism, treaties & pathways to change

4 activities·Est. 4 hrs·Visual & discussion
Overview
TPNW reading
Case studies
Discussion
Action mapping
Today's focus

From understanding to action

Days 1–3 built your understanding of the problem. Today we look at how change actually happens — through treaties, movements, and individual action — and begin mapping your own pathway.

TPNW reading — Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons (primary source)
Case studies — The 1980s freeze movement & ICAN's Nobel campaign
Discussion — What makes activism effective?
Action mapping — Draft your civic action pathway for Day 5
Key frameworks

Treaty-based change

How international law constrains (and sometimes changes) state behavior — and who drives those treaties.

Movement-based change

From SANE to the Nuclear Freeze to ICAN — how civil society moves policy.

Individual action

Writing, organizing, voting, running for office — the concrete steps available to any student.

Primary source — TPNW

Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons (2021)

Read Articles 1–4 of the TPNW. Focus on: what states agree to prohibit, how verification works, and which major powers have not signed.

🔗 Read the full TPNW → disarmament.unoda.org
Reading response

What surprised you about the treaty? What do you think explains why the US, Russia, and China have not signed?

Case studies — movements that moved policy

The Nuclear Freeze Movement (1980s)

One million people rallied in Central Park in 1982. Within a decade, the US and USSR had signed the INF Treaty. How did a student-led movement become a global force — and what were its limits?

🔗 Listen: Walk in the Park podcast → atthebrink.org

ICAN's Nobel Prize campaign

The International Campaign to Abolish Nuclear Weapons won the 2017 Nobel Peace Prize three years after the TPNW negotiations began. A coalition of NGOs, not governments, drove the process. What does this tell us about where power sits?

🔗 ICAN Nobel Prize campaign → icanw.org
Discussion question

Which lever for change do you find most credible after today's case studies?

International treaties and legal frameworks
Grassroots movements and public pressure
Electoral politics — voting and running for office
Media and cultural change — shifting how people think
Discussion prompts

How do these levers interact? Can one work without the others? Where do you see yourself fitting in?

Action mapping — prepare for Day 5
Assignment

Draft an outline of your Day 5 civic action brief. Identify: (1) one specific problem from the course, (2) one lever for change, (3) one concrete step you or your community could take, (4) one source of evidence from the course.

Your outline

This is a working draft — you'll develop it into a 2-page brief for Day 5.

← Course home

Day 5 — Your civic action plan

3 activities·Est. 4 hrs·Hands-on & presentations
Overview
Finalize brief
Presentations
Course close

Today: from learning to doing

You've spent four days understanding the problem. Today you present your answer: one concrete, evidence-based step toward reducing nuclear risk — and make the case for why it matters.

Finalize — Complete your 2-page civic action brief
Present — 3-minute presentations to the class (with Q&A)
Close — Final reflection and course wrap-up
Civic action brief — final draft
Brief requirements (2 pages)

1. Identify one specific, current nuclear risk
2. Name one realistic lever for change (treaty, movement, policy, media)
3. Propose one concrete step you or your community could take
4. Cite at least two sources from the course
5. End with a call to action for your classmates

Final draft

Write your complete brief here or on paper to hand in.

Presentations

3-minute presentations

Each student presents their civic action brief to the class. After each presentation, the class has 2 minutes for questions. Your facilitator will keep time.

Peer feedback prompt

As you listen to each presentation: What is the most compelling part of their argument? What question would you want them to explore further?

Course close
Final reflection

Compared to Day 1, how has your sense of personal agency on nuclear issues changed?

Much more — I now see concrete things I can do
Somewhat more — I understand better but still feel uncertain
About the same — the scale of the problem still feels overwhelming
Less — learning more made me feel less hopeful
Final discussion — no wrong answers

What is the one thing you'll take from this week?

Final written reflection

What is one thing you'll actually do differently after this course?

Take action

Take action — organizations

Learning is only the beginning. These organizations work every day to reduce nuclear risk and build a safer world. Explore, engage, and consider how you can contribute.

Photo placeholder
  • 🌐
    International Campaign to Abolish Nuclear Weapons (ICAN)Nobel Prize–winning coalition working toward a global treaty to ban nuclear weapons.
    ↗

    ↗

    ↗

    ↗

Facilitator guide — for instructors & group leaders

Everything you need to run the course

Scripts, timing blocks, emotional safety protocols, and activity notes for each day. Select a day below.

Day 1
Day 2 — Art
Day 3
Day 4
Day 5
⏰
Day 1 — Timing overview
~5 hrs
9:00 am
Welcome, grounding exercise, opt-out reminder
30 min
9:30 am
Pre-reading — The Button Ch. 1 + free write
60 min
11:00 am
Film screening — How to Stop a Nuclear War
90 min
2:00 pm
Discussion questions (MCQ + class debrief)
45 min
2:45 pm
Anonymous poll + written reflection
20 min
👤
Opening grounding script
Read aloud
Opening script (word for word)

"Before we begin, I want to name something. The topic we're exploring this week is serious — it touches on fear, uncertainty, and real global stakes. It's okay to feel uncomfortable. There's no pressure to share anything you're not ready to share. If you need to step out at any point, please do. And if something comes up for you emotionally, come find me."

Follow with: 3 minutes of quiet breathing. Ask students to put phones away and take three slow breaths with you. No discussion yet.

⚠
Film debrief — handling emotional responses
Important

After the film, pause for 2 minutes of silence before opening discussion. Do not rush to fill the quiet.

If students seem overwhelmed: "It's okay if this brought something up. You don't have to respond to everything right now. Let's take a breath together."

If a student disengages: Don't call on them. Allow written responses as an alternative to speaking. The anonymous poll is specifically designed to give quieter students (Jason-type personas) a low-stakes entry point.

If misinformation surfaces: "That's a perspective I've heard — let's look at what the film specifically says and then compare." Redirect to evidence, not correction.

💡
Discussion facilitation tips
Pedagogy

The MCQ questions are designed so students answer privately first — this gives every student an opinion before peer pressure sets in.

Recommended sequence: (1) Students answer individually, (2) Reveal class results on screen, (3) Ask: "What surprised you about the results?" before opening the floor.

Quieter students: Offer a "write first, then share" option. Ask them to write one sentence response before you call on anyone. This approach is especially effective for engineering/technical students who may feel less comfortable in debate formats.

⏰
Day 2 — Timing overview (Atomic Art in America)
~3 hrs
9:00 am
Warm-up discussion — media representations of nuclear war
20 min
9:20 am
Opening framing — Abstraction in the Atomic Age
10 min
9:30 am
NYT Article (1945) — read + 5 min free write
40 min
10:10 am
Artwork slideshow — 3 pieces (Peterdi, Dehner, Moholy-Nagy)
45 min
10:55 am
Full class discussion — 7 guiding questions
30 min
11:25 am
Exit ticket — written or sketch
10 min
👤
Opening framing script
Read aloud
Opening script

"We've been living under the threat of nuclear war for over 80 years. The examples you just mentioned reflect modern interpretations — but to understand how people first processed this threat, we can look at how artists responded during the early Atomic Age. After the bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki in 1945, people in the United States grappled with a lot of uncertainty and fear. Much of the art from the 1940s–1960s turned toward abstraction as a means to express emotions that were difficult to put into words. Abstraction means using distorted forms, surreal imagery, and biomorphic shapes — forms that suggest living things without being literal."

🎨
Artwork slideshow instructions — all 3 pieces
Activity guide

For each artwork, follow this sequence:

  • Show the image without any title, artist, or context
  • Ask students: "What do you see? What do you feel? What questions come up?"
  • Allow 2–3 minutes of open sharing before revealing context
  • Provide the artist biography and interpretations
  • Ask: "Does knowing the context change how you see it?"

Artwork introduction script:

Say before each artwork

"Now let's see how American artists responded to the same event. I'll show each piece first without any context. I'll ask you to share what you see, what you feel, and any questions that come up."

📄
Artist notes — Gabor Peterdi, Nervous Lobster (1947)

Peterdi was a Hungarian-American artist who studied in Italy before immigrating to the US in 1939. He enlisted in the US Army and fought in Europe in WWII, then established the Graphic Workshop at the Brooklyn Museum Art School.

This piece used etching and gaufrage (embossing on metal plates) on zinc. His work explores vulnerability and tension.

  • The distorted lobster = symbol of nature's fragility under technological pressure
  • Mechanical quality = unease about technology and nature becoming entangled
  • Red coloring = anxiety about technology's increasing influence on society
📄
Artist notes — Dorothy Dehner, Woman #2 (1954)

Dehner was an American painter and sculptor born in 1901 in Cleveland. Her early work focused on daily life, but after her divorce in 1950 she became more professionally active. She studied printmaking at Hayter's Atelier 17 and focused on personal stories in the Atomic Age.

  • Meant to express internal psychological impact rather than external threat
  • Represents a human-centered response to the Atomic Age
  • Fragmented, looping forms = tension between identity and social instability
📄
Artist notes — László Moholy-Nagy, Nuclear II (1946)

Moholy-Nagy was a Hungarian painter and photographer, influenced by constructivism. He advocated for integrating technology into the arts. This painting was made while he was recovering from cancer, in direct response to Hiroshima and Nagasaki.

  • More structured, possibly optimistic reading of nuclear energy as progress
  • White squares/triangles suggest missile-like shapes
  • Vibrant colors may celebrate nuclear technology — but raises: can we separate nuclear power from nuclear weapons?

Note: Dimensions of this work are still being confirmed — update before class.

💬
Full class discussion — 7 guiding questions
Pick 3–4

Recommended format: Turn and talk (2 min) → full class (15–20 min). Option 2: full class from the start.

  • What is the overall tone and message of the 1945 NYT article?
  • How does that tone compare to the emotions expressed in the artworks?
  • How do you think you would have reacted to this article at the time? To the artwork?
  • Why might the media and artists portray the same event so differently?
  • In what ways is art more effective (or less effective) than journalism at conveying emotional truth?
  • How do you think people in 1945 might have reacted to this article versus these artworks?
  • Do you think nuclear war is over-sensationalized, underrepresented, or misunderstood in today's media?
📋
Exit ticket — instructions

Pass around exit tickets (index cards or printed slips). Collect as students leave. Two options:

  • Option 1 (written): "After today's discussion, has your perception of nuclear war changed?"
  • Option 2 (sketch): "Draw a quick sketch of your interpretation of one concept or artwork from today."

Exit tickets do not need to be graded — use them to gauge emotional state and comprehension going into Day 3.

⚠
Facilitator personal notes (from curriculum draft)
For your awareness

The following concerns were flagged during curriculum development — worth keeping in mind:

  • Art interpretation is inherently subjective — frame all interpretations as "possible readings," not definitive answers
  • Dimensions for Nuclear II (Moholy-Nagy) still need to be confirmed before class
  • Consider whether to add a 4th artwork — the current three are all abstract, which accurately reflects the era but may benefit from one more grounded example
  • More research on Moholy-Nagy's personal relationship to the bombings may strengthen the contextual discussion
⏰
Day 3 — Timing overview
~4 hrs
9:00 am
Reading — Command and Control excerpt (Damascus incident)
45 min
9:45 am
Film clip — facilitator-selected segment on deterrence
20 min
10:05 am
MCQ discussion — ethics of deterrence
40 min
10:45 am
Short essay (300 words) — graded
60 min
⚠
Emotional safety note for Day 3
Important

Day 3 engages with moral philosophy under conditions of mass violence. Some students may find the ethical framing destabilizing — particularly those from communities historically affected by nuclear testing (Marshall Islands, Navajo Nation, downwinders in the American West).

Before the discussion: "I want to acknowledge that for some communities, nuclear weapons are not an abstract policy question — they are lived experience. If anything today brings up something personal, you are welcome to step back or speak with me privately."

💡
Discussion tips — deterrence ethics

The deterrence ethics question is intentionally open — there is no single correct answer. Resist the urge to resolve the tension. Your role is to keep the conversation evidence-based and ensure no position is mocked.

If a student says "this is just how it is": "What would it take for that to change? Has it ever changed in history?"

If a student expresses hopelessness: "Let's hold that feeling — we're going to look at Day 4 specifically for evidence that change is possible."

⏰
Day 4 — Timing overview
~4 hrs
9:00 am
TPNW reading + response
40 min
9:40 am
Case studies — Freeze movement & ICAN
30 min
10:10 am
MCQ discussion — levers for change
40 min
10:50 am
Action mapping — Day 5 brief outline
60 min
💡
Facilitation note — moving from problem to agency

Day 4 is the emotional turning point of the course. After two days of heavy content, students need to feel that action is possible. Be intentional about your energy — if you seem tired or cynical, the room will reflect it.

Opening reframe: "Yesterday we sat with a hard question. Today we look at what people have actually done — and what you might do."

The action mapping activity is the most important hands-on work of the week. Give students genuine time (45–60 min) to develop their outlines. Circulate and ask questions rather than giving answers.

⏰
Day 5 — Timing overview
~4 hrs
9:00 am
Brief finalization (individual writing time)
60 min
10:00 am
Presentations (3 min each + 2 min Q&A)
90 min
11:30 am
Final MCQ poll + written reflection
20 min
11:50 am
Closing remarks & course wrap-up
15 min
👤
Closing script
Read aloud
Closing script

"This week you engaged with one of the most complex problems in human history — not to paralyze you, but to give you the tools to act. You've done something most people never do: you sat with the discomfort long enough to understand it. That's where change begins. Whatever you do next — one conversation, one letter, one vote, one op-ed — it matters. Thank you for being here."
